title: Sudden sky-high liver enzymes after a cardiac arrest

chart.oneLiner: 68M with heart failure, admitted after a witnessed cardiac arrest and prolonged hypotension, now with markedly elevated transaminases noted on labs 24 hours later.

opening: I'm wiped out and a bit short of breath since my heart stopped yesterday — now the nurses say my liver numbers are way off.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man, fatigued and mildly breathless, recovering on a monitored floor after resuscitation. Cooperative but tires easily; defers technical detail to the chart.

HPI: Yesterday he collapsed at home with a witnessed cardiac arrest from his known heart failure; bystander CPR, EMS found him in a low-output state and he was profoundly hypotensive (SBP in the 60s) for roughly 30–45 minutes before perfusion was restored. Since then he has been weak, slightly breathless, and mildly nauseated, with reduced urine output overnight (only if asked). NO abdominal pain to speak of, NO right-upper-quadrant pain, NO dark urine, NO fevers or chills, NO recent travel, NO new sexual contacts, NO IV drug use. He has NOT taken any extra acetaminophen and denies any large overdose (only if asked about Tylenol/paracetamol/overdose). NO heavy alcohol use (a beer at holidays). NO prior liver disease, NO known hepatitis, NO blood transfusions, NO mushroom ingestion or new herbal supplements.
PMH: ischemic cardiomyopathy with HFrEF (EF ~25%), prior MI, CKD stage 3, hypertension, type 2 diabetes. MEDS: furosemide, carvedilol, lisinopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, metformin; occasional acetaminophen for knees (a couple of tablets, not recently). SOCIAL: retired, lives with wife, minimal alcohol, never smoker, no recreational drugs.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired, mildly breathless, no acute distress at rest; not jaundiced.
- Vitals: BP 92/58, HR 104, RR 20, 36.8°C, SpO2 94% on 2L; orthostatic/marginal perfusion if checked.
- Cardiac: elevated JVP, soft S3 gallop; cool extremities with delayed capillary refill if examined; thready peripheral pulses.
- Abdomen: soft; mild non-tender hepatomegaly possible; NO RUQ tenderness, NO Murphy sign, NO ascites.
- Extremities: mild lower-limb edema; cool to touch.
- Neuro: alert and oriented; no asterixis.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the duration of hypotension, the low urine output, or the acetaminophen detail unless asked about the arrest/blood pressure, urine output, or medications/overdose respectively.
- Stigmata of low cardiac output (cool extremities, delayed capillary refill, elevated JVP, S3) and hepatomegaly are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.LFTs: AST 4200, ALT 3100 — massive transaminase elevation (>25–50x ULN) that rose rapidly and on repeat 48–72h later falls rapidly toward baseline once perfusion restored; alkaline phosphatase only mildly up.
labs.Bilirubin: Total bilirubin mildly elevated (~2.5 mg/dL) — disproportionately low relative to the huge transaminases.
labs.LDH: LDH markedly elevated; ALT:LDH ratio LOW (<1.5) — favors ischemic over viral injury.
labs.Acetaminophen: Acetaminophen level UNDETECTABLE (excludes APAP toxicity — the key mimic).
labs.ViralHepNAT: Hepatitis A IgM, B surface antigen/core IgM, and C viral load all NEGATIVE (excludes acute viral hepatitis).
labs.RenalCoags: Cr risen from baseline (acute kidney injury); INR transiently prolonged; lactate elevated — consistent with the same hypoperfusion insult.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves (prior MI); no acute ST-elevation.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves (prior MI); no acute ST-elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia, low voltage, old anteroseptal Q waves
searchTerms: old anteroseptal infarction Q waves ECG

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular redistribution and small bilateral effusions — pulmonary edema/congestion.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular redistribution and small bilateral effusions — pulmonary edema/congestion. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph cardiomegaly pulmonary edema small effusions

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for these markedly elevated transaminases (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) (leading,7). Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity (CANT-MISS to exclude,5). Acute viral hepatitis (CANT-MISS,3). Acute Budd-Chiari/venous outflow obstruction (CANT-MISS,3). Autoimmune hepatitis / drug-induced (toxin) injury (core,2). Acute biliary obstruction (core,2). Names the picture as hypoperfusion injury — massive transaminases with low ALT:LDH ratio after a hemodynamic insult (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity; Acute viral hepatitis; Acute Budd-Chiari/venous outflow obstruction. Also reasonable: Autoimmune/drug-induced (toxin) hepatitis; Acute biliary obstruction.

coreActions.item: Names the picture as hypoperfusion injury (massive transaminases with low ALT:LDH ratio after a hemodynamic insult)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio) (CANT-MISS,3). Acetaminophen level (CANT-MISS,3). Viral hepatitis serologies/NAT (2). Bilirubin and INR/albumin — synthetic function (2). Renal function and lactate — assess the perfusion insult (2). RUQ ultrasound with Doppler — exclude Budd-Chiari/biliary obstruction (2). Repeat LFTs to confirm rapid downtrend (1). PENALTY: anchoring on shock liver without checking an acetaminophen level / excluding viral causes (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio); Acetaminophen level; RUQ ultrasound with Doppler.

criticalActions.item: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio)

penalties.item: anchoring on shock liver without checking an acetaminophen level / excluding viral causes

requiredTests: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio)
contraindicatedTests: anchoring on shock liver without checking an acetaminophen level / excluding viral causes
orderSensitiveActions: anchoring on shock liver without checking an acetaminophen level / excluding viral causes

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, low voltage, old anteroseptal Q waves, no acute STEMI. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes old infarct/low voltage and absence of acute ST-elevation (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling an acute STEMI / missing the old infarct (-3).
keyFindings: sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage
old anteroseptal Q waves without acute ST-elevation
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation.
commonMistakes: calling the old anteroseptal Q waves an acute STEMI
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves from prior MI; no acute ST-elevation.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation.
coreActions: sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage
old anteroseptal Q waves without acute ST-elevation

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = cardiomegaly with pulmonary congestion. CREDIT: identifies cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/effusions (6); links congestion to the low-output state driving the liver injury (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / inventing a pneumonia (-2).
keyFindings: small bilateral effusions consistent with congestion
criticalFindings: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small effusions.
commonMistakes: mistaking pulmonary congestion for multifocal pneumonia
steps.idealAnswer: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular redistribution and small bilateral effusions — cardiogenic congestion, consistent with the low-output state.

criticalActions: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small effusions.
coreActions: small bilateral effusions consistent with congestion

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Massive transaminases (AST 4200/ALT 3100) with disproportionately mild bilirubin, markedly elevated LDH with a low ALT:LDH ratio, undetectable acetaminophen, negative viral serologies, AKI/elevated lactate, and a documented period of profound hypotension. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) (6). Cites the hypoperfusion insult with massive transaminases, low ALT:LDH ratio, and disproportionately mild bilirubin; explicitly excludes acetaminophen toxicity (undetectable level) and acute viral hepatitis (negative serologies), and expects a rapid downtrend with restored perfusion (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver): the hypoperfusion insult explains the massive transaminases, low ALT:LDH ratio, and disproportionately mild bilirubin, and the LFTs should downtrend rapidly once perfusion is restored. Ruled out acetaminophen toxicity (undetectable level) and acute viral hepatitis (negative serologies).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH acute liver injury / shock physiology: restore and optimize hepatic perfusion — treat the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state (CANT-MISS,7); supportive care and serial LFT/INR/lactate monitoring expecting rapid improvement (4); avoid/adjust hepatotoxic and renally-cleared drugs, hold nephrotoxins (4); treat precipitants and decompensated heart failure with cardiology input (4); monitor for and manage acute kidney injury and hypoglycemia (3); hepatology consult and avoid unnecessary steroids/empiric NAC unless APAP not yet excluded (3). PENALTY: giving fluids/pressors blindly without addressing the cardiac low-output cause, worsening congestion (-3); attributing the liver injury to a primary hepatic disease and delaying hemodynamic resuscitation (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: The liver injury is a marker of hypoperfusion — the priority is to restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output heart failure (inotropic/afterload support as appropriate, optimize cardiac output, correct hypoxemia and anemia) with cardiology. Provide supportive care with serial LFTs, INR, and lactate, expecting a rapid downtrend. Avoid and dose-adjust hepatotoxic/renally-cleared drugs, hold nephrotoxins, and monitor for AKI and hypoglycemia. Hepatology consult; avoid empiric steroids and reserve NAC for cases where acetaminophen has not been excluded.

criticalActions.item: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state

coreActions.item: Supportive care with serial LFT/INR/lactate monitoring
Avoid/adjust hepatotoxic and renally-cleared drugs; hold nephrotoxins
Treat precipitants and decompensated heart failure with cardiology input
Monitor for and manage AKI and hypoglycemia

bonusActions.item: Reserve NAC for cases where acetaminophen has not been excluded

penalties.item: Fluids/pressors without addressing the cardiac low-output cause (worsening congestion)

requiredActions: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
optionalActions: Supportive care with serial LFT/INR/lactate monitoring
Avoid/adjust hepatotoxic and renally-cleared drugs; hold nephrotoxins
Treat precipitants and decompensated heart failure with cardiology input
Monitor for and manage AKI and hypoglycemia
Reserve NAC for cases where acetaminophen has not been excluded
unsafeActions: Fluids/pressors without addressing the cardiac low-output cause (worsening congestion)

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with heart failure, admitted after a witnessed cardiac arrest and prolonged hypotension, now with markedly elevated transaminases noted on labs 24 hours later. The diagnosis is Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver), reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Vitals: BP 92/58, HR 104, marginal perfusion; persistent low-output state on reassessment.
Cardiac/extremities: cool extremities with delayed capillary refill and thready peripheral pulses — low-output perfusion.
Cardiac: elevated JVP with a soft S3 gallop — volume overload/low-output heart failure.
Abdomen: soft with mild non-tender hepatomegaly; NO RUQ tenderness, NO Murphy sign, NO ascites.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Fluids/pressors without addressing the cardiac low-output cause (worsening congestion)
managementPearls: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
criticalWorkupItems: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio)
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation.
Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small effusions.
criticalManagementItems: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation.
Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small effusions.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
